sentence: Subjective:
HPI: The patient, Larry, has been experiencing chronic back pain for approximately 2 years, which started after lifting furniture one weekend.
The pain is described as a sharp stabbing pain in the back that radiates down to the right leg, specifically to the big toe.
The patient also experiences numbness and tingling in the right leg, especially to the foot.
He denies any loss of sensation in the genital or rectal area, weakness, or loss of control of bladder or bowels.
He has tried physical therapy and strong pain medications in the past, but has not found relief.
Past medical history: The patient has no known family members with spine conditions and does not smoke.
He quit smoking 20 years ago.
Review of systems: The patient reports difficulty being active due to back pain, which has led to weight gain over the years.
He also mentions that his back pain has prevented him from enjoying his recent hobby of golf.
Current medications: [NOT MENTIONED]
Objective:
Vital signs: [NOT MENTIONED]
Physical exam: The musculoskeletal back exam revealed no overlying erythema or ecchymosis.
There is midline tenderness at the L4-L5 disk space with right-sided lumbar paravertebral tenderness.
The patient experiences pain with lumbar flexion and a positive supine straight leg test.
The neurological examination revealed decreased sensation to light touch at the right lateral thigh at L4 dermatome.
Test Results: The MRI results show disk herniation with nerve impingement.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with an L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy, which is causing tingling in the leg.
Problem list: L4-L5 herniated disk with radiculopathy; chronic lumbar back pain.
Plan: I recommended a spinal injection with a corticosteroid to help reduce inflammation and alleviate pain.
The procedure will be performed under fluoroscopy, and the patient will be monitored for response to the treatment.
The patient will not require long-term treatment, but rather intermittent injections as needed, not more than 4 times a year.
Follow-up: The patient will be scheduled for the first injection today, and subsequent injections will be scheduled as needed based on response to treatment.
